964294.summary: This shrubland association is known from Glen Canyon National Recreation Area and likely occurs more widely in the Colorado Plateau. It is characterized by an open to moderately dense shrub canopy layer dominated by Chrysothamnus viscidiflorus. Associated shrubs with sparse to low cover include Amelanchier utahensis, Artemisia tridentata, Atriplex canescens, Ephedra viridis, Gutierrezia sarothrae, Purshia mexicana, and Purshia tridentata. Because of disturbance, the herbaceous layer often has moderately high cover and is dominated by exotic Bromus tectorum with other weedy species such as Lappula occidentalis, Machaeranthera canescens, and Salsola tragus. Other species usually present with low to moderate cover include native grasses Bouteloua gracilis, Elymus trachycaulus, Leymus salinus, Muhlenbergia asperifolia, and Poa fendleriana. Pinus edulis and Juniperus osteosperma trees may have low cover on the margins of the community. Total vegetation cover in these disturbed shrublands ranges from 9 to 52%. Stands occur on disturbed sites on mesatops, ridges, canyon bottoms, basins, and along intermittent streams between 1522 and 2258 m elevation. Sites are flat to gently sloping with west and northwest aspects. Soils are sands and silt loams derived from alluvial or eolian deposits, from shales or sandstones. The unvegetated surface is dominated by bare soil, with lesser cover of litter.

972805.scientificName: Distichlis spicata - Juncus arcticus ssp. littoralis Alkaline Wet Meadow

1013677.scientificName: Calamagrostis canadensis - Mixed Forb-Graminoid Meadow Alliance

1093904.scientificName: Alnus incana - Calamagrostis canadensis Laurentian-Acadian Wet Meadow & Shrub Swamp Group
1093904.conceptSentence: This Laurentian-Acadian group is found in basins or along rivers and streams in areas of low current where soils are saturated or flooded with shallow water for part to most of the growing season. The vegetation is dominated by short to medium-tall forbs and graminoids and short to medium tall shrubs.
1093904.summary: This group is found in the Laurentian-Acadian region of the Great Lakes and the northeastern United States and adjacent Canada. It is characterized by wet-mesic to wet sites and can be dominated by a variety of graminoids and forbs. Common shrubs include Alnus incana, Cornus sericea, and Ilex mucronata. Common abundant species include the graminoids Calamagrostis canadensis,Calamagrostis stricta, Carex lacustris, Carex stricta, Carex utriculata, Glyceria striata, Leersia oryzoides, and Scirpus cyperinus. Forbs that may be common or dominant include Eutrochium fistulosum, Eupatorium perfoliatum, Impatiens capensis, Impatiens pallida, Sagittaria latifolia, Solidago rugosa, Symphyotrichum racemosum, and Verbesina alternifolia. Sites are found in basins or along slow-moving rivers and streams and are flooded or saturated for at least some of the growing season but rarely more than 0.3 m deep.

1159085.scientificName: Alnus incana / Spartina pectinata - Deschampsia cespitosa Riverscour Vegetation Group
1159085.conceptSentence: This group consists of sparsely vegetated barren riverscour, both outcrop-pavement and unconsolidated sand, gravel, and cobble, in the Great Lakes, Laurentian, and northeastern North America regions, from Pennsylvania to Québec, Canada and west to Ontario and Wisconsin. Dominants include Calamagrostis canadensis, Campanula rotundifolia, Deschampsia cespitosa, Doellingeria umbellata, Hypericum prolificum, Packera paupercula, Prunus pumila, Solidago simplex, and Spartina pectinata, and/or Triantha glutinosa. Sites are frequently affected by flooding or ice-scour but are not flooded for most of the growing season.
1159085.summary: This group consists of sparsely vegetated barren riverscour, both outcrop-pavement and unconsolidated sand, gravel and cobble, in the Great Lakes, Laurentian, and northeastern North America regions, from Pennsylvania to Québec, Canada and west to Ontario and Wisconsin. Species composition is highly variable because of riverscouring and variable substrates. Characteristic species include Andropogon gerardii, Calamagrostis canadensis, Campanula rotundifolia, Deschampsia cespitosa, Doellingeria umbellata, Hypericum prolificum, Packera paupercula, Prunus pumila, Solidago simplex, Spartina pectinata, and/or Triantha glutinosa.

1170368.scientificName: Pinus contorta / Arctostaphylos uva-ursi / Cladina spp. Alliance

1222763.scientificName: Arctostaphylos (nevadensis, uva-ursi) Dwarf-shrubland & Montane Bald Alliance
1222763.summary: This alliance consists of vegetated montane balds of the East Cascades dominated by mat-forming Arctostaphylos nevadensis or, less frequently, A. uva-ursi. Calamagrostis rubescens, Pseudoroegneria spicata, and Paxistima myrsinites are characteristic associates. These communities occur within a matrix of mixed Pinus ponderosa-Pseudotsuga menziesii forests and woodlands.

1222793.conceptSentence: Sparsely vegetated high montane and subalpine balds with Lewisia columbiana as the most abundant herb
1222793.summary: This association occurs on steep (30° mean), high montane and subalpine balds with southwest-facing aspects (214° mean). It also occurs on relatively, flat upper bald positions. Lewisia columbiana is the most abundant species in a poorly developed herb layer. Juncus parryi is usually prominent, but may be absent. A sparse dwarf-shrub layer may contain Paxistima myrsinites and Arctostaphylos nevadensis. Common associate herbs include Sedum divergens, Saxifraga ferruginea, Lomatium brandegeei, Danthonia intermedia, Festuca viridula, Eremogone (=Arenaria) capillaris and Phlox diffusa. Selaginella wallacei may be prominent. Lomatium martindalei is absent.

1222805.scientificName: Arctostaphylos uva-ursi - Fragaria virginiana - (Festuca idahoensis ssp. roemeri) Dwarf-shrubland
1222805.conceptSentence: Low- to mid-elevation balds and occasionally prairies dominated by Arctostaphylos uva-ursi.
1222805.summary: These low- to mid-elevation dwarf-shrublands are dominated by Arctostaphylos uva-ursi (always with at least 25% cover). Juniperus communis, Penstemon davidsonii, and Arctostaphylos nevadensis are absent. Festuca roemeri is usually present in the Olympics and Puget Lowlands, and sometimes codominant. Fragaria virginiana is usually present or prominent. Eriophyllum lanatum, Luzula multiflora and Zigadenus venenosus are often present. Calamagrostis rubescens and Danthonia intermedia are rarely prominent (near Ross Lake in the North Cascades). Racomitrium canescens is frequently absent, but is prominent where it occurs. Stands occur on balds and occasionally on glacial outwash prairies.

1222851.conceptSentence: Juncus parryi dominates in rocky parklands and on open ridges.
1222851.summary: These subalpine (1650-1900m) herbaceous communities occurs on moderate slopes (18° mean) with southerly aspects (191° mean) and rocky soils. Juncus parryi dominates this herbaceous community. Antennaria lanata, Hieracium triste, Phlox diffusa, Festuca roemeri, Eremogone (=Arenaria) capillaris, Lupinus latifolius, Luetkea pectinata, Bistorta (=Polygonum) bistortoides, and Carex spectabilis are frequent to occasionally prominent, but are never more abundant than Juncus parryi.

1270115.scientificName: North American Boreal Alnus viridis - Salix glauca Shrubland & Grassland Macrogroup
1270115.conceptSentence: This macrogroup occurs across much of the North American boreal region in continental climates, varying from shrublands to grasslands, herbaceous meadows, and mosaics of the types. Species vary considerably in cover and composition due to the diverse ecological drivers that produce this type of habitat, including both stand replacing disturbances at forest and woodland sites, where shrubland and grasslands represent mid-successional vegetation, and site conditions are severe enough to prevent tree establishment.
1270115.summary: This macrogroup occurs across much of the North American boreal region in continental climates and varies from shrublands, grasslands, and herbaceous meadows, or mosaics of the types. Shrublands vary considerably in cover and composition due to the diverse ecological drivers that produce this type of habitat. Following stand replacing disturbances at forest and woodland sites, shrubland and grasslands represent mid-successional vegetation, and the occurrence of tree seedlings and saplings typically increases as the community develops. Stands are characterized by one or more of the following deciduous shrub species: Alnus alnobetula ssp. fruticosa, Salix pulchra, Salix glauca, Salix richardsonii, Betula glandulosa, and/or Betula nana. More productive, alder-dominated sites typically support an herb-rich understory including Calamagrostis canadensis, Chamaenerion angustifolium, Equisetum arvense, Gymnocarpium dryopteris, Dryopteris expansa, and others. These moist site herbs can also occur in species-rich meadows interspersed with the shrublands in areas of late-lying snow. Less productive sites are birch-willow dominated. Occasionally, boreal shrubland and grasslands also develop on inland dunes and steppe bluffs. Biological soil crust and lichens are important mechanisms for the stabilization of dune and bluff soils. This type occurs in upland settings where extreme site conditions produce shrub or herb vegetation within a climate that would otherwise support forest. This macrogroup develops in uplands where extreme site conditions preclude the establishment of trees. Site conditions may be generalized as those related to landscape position and those related to disturbance. With respect to landscape position, alders form continuous bands in montane settings above treeline, birch and willow form thickets at high-elevations and along cold air drainages, and meadows occupy areas of late-lying snow. Excessively drained and easily erodible sands of inland dunes favor the establishment of rhizomatous graminoids and shrubs over trees. With respect to disturbance, boreal shrubland and grasslands may develop on sites temporarily returned to an early successional stage by wildland fire, or held in early succession by slope instability, non-cohesive soils, or near constant wind.
1270115.macrogroup: North American Boreal Alnus viridis - Salix glauca Shrubland & Grassland Macrogroup

1270948.scientificName: Purshia tridentata - Artemisia tridentata Desert Shrubland Alliance
1270948.primaryCommonName: Antelope Bitterbrush - Big Sagebrush Desert Shrubland

1275202.scientificName: Artemisia tridentata / Pascopyrum smithii Shrub Grassland Alliance
1275202.conceptSentence: This shrubland type is dominated by Artemisia tridentata spp. wyomingensis andassociated with graminoids characteristic of mixedgrass prairie.
1275202.summary: This shrubland type is dominated by Artemisia tridentata spp. wyomingensis with an understory composed of midgrasses typical of the Great Plains of Montana and western North Dakota's mixedgrass prairies, such as Bouteloua gracilis, Pascopyrum smithii, Hesperostipa comata, Koeleria macrantha, Pseudoroegneria spicata, and Poa secunda. Forbs are also typical of mixedgrass prairies but may include species associated with the eastern Rocky Mountain slopes (e.g., Lupinus sericeus). Sites occur on slopes and plains over clay to sandy loam soils.

1275208.scientificName: Carex pellita - Carex spp. - Calamagrostis canadensis Great Plains Wet Meadow Alliance
1275208.conceptSentence: This wet meadow alliance is found throughout the Great Plains on flat or depressional sites where the water table is at or just below the surface for at least part of the growing season. Typical tall graminoid dominants include Carex pellita, Carex atherodes, Carex aquatilis, Carex stricta, Carex inexpansa, Calamagrostis stricta, Calamagrostis canadensis or Juncus balticus.
1275208.summary: This wet meadow alliance is found throughout the Great Plains on flat or depressional sites where the water table is at or near the surface for at least part of the growing season. Typical tall graminoid dominants include Carex pellita, Carex atherodes, Carex aquatilis, Carex stricta, and Carex inexpansa. Codominants include Calamagrostis stricta, Calamagrostis canadensis, Juncus balticus, or Schoenoplectus spp. Forb cover is low to moderate, including species such as Alisma triviale (= Alisma plantago-aquatica var. americanum), Symphyotrichum lanceolatum, Sium suave, Lycopus asper, Epilobium spp., and Potentilla anserina. Shrubs (Salix spp.) may be present but only with sparse cover. Sites are very productive for grazing but soils are susceptible to pugging and hummocking by cattle hooves.

1285582.scientificName: Central Rocky Mountain Amelanchier alnifolia - Menziesia ferruginea - Physocarpus malvaceus Shrubland Macrogroup
1285582.conceptSentence: Shrublands and shrub-steppe included in this macrogroup are found throughout the central Rocky Mountain region, west to the eastern Cascades and eastern Sierra Nevada. Vegetation is mostly deciduous broadleaf shrubs, sometimes mixed with shrub-statured trees or sparse evergreen needleleaf trees, and composition is highly variable. Stands occur in foothills below treeline and as patches within the continuous forest in the montane and subalpine zones where environmental conditions restrict tree growth.
1285582.summary: Shrublands and shrub-steppe included in this macrogroup are found in the Columbia Basin and throughout the Central Rocky Mountains from Wyoming north and west into British Columbia and Alberta, including east into central Montana around the "Sky Island" ranges. It also occurs farther south into central and eastern Wyoming and north along the foothills of the Front Range into Alberta and in the East Cascades and northern Sierra Nevada. Vegetation is mostly deciduous broadleaf shrubs, sometimes mixed with shrub-statured trees or sparse evergreen needleleaf trees and Populus tremuloides, especially in avalanche chutes. Also included are dwarf-shrublands dominated by Arctostaphylos nevadensis, Arctostaphylos uva-ursi, Juniperus communis, Paxistima myrsinites, Vaccinium cespitosum, Vaccinium myrtillus, and Vaccinium scoparium. Common dominant shrubs are Acer glabrum, Amelanchier alnifolia, Ceanothus velutinus, Dasiphora fruticosa, Holodiscus discolor, Philadelphus lewisii, Physocarpus malvaceus, Prunus emarginata, Prunus virginiana, Rhus glabra, Rhus trilobata, Rosa nutkana, Rosa woodsii, Symphoricarpos albus, and Symphoricarpos oreophilus, occurring alone or in any combination. Stands in central and eastern Wyoming can include Artemisia tridentata ssp. vaseyana and Cercocarpus montanus, but neither of these species are dominant, and where they occur the stands are truly mixes of shrubs, often with Amelanchier alnifolia, Prunus virginiana, and others being the predominant taxa. Higher elevation stands include, Menziesia ferruginea, Ribes lacustre, Rubus parviflorus, Salix scouleriana, Sorbus sitchensis, Spiraea betulifolia, Spiraea splendens, and Vaccinium membranaceum. The herbaceous layer is typically composed of graminoids such as Aristida purpurea, Calamagrostis rubescens, Carex geyeri, Deschampsia cespitosa, Festuca campestris, Festuca idahoensis, Koeleria macrantha, Poa secunda, and Pseudoroegneria spicata. Achnatherum thurberianum and Leymus cinereus can be locally important. Balsamorhiza sagittata, Geum triflorum, Lomatium triternatum, Oenanthe sarmentosa, Potentilla gracilis, and species of Eriogonum, Phlox, and Erigeron are important forbs. Other important graminoids and forbs include Chamerion angustifolium, Luzula glabrata var. hitchcockii, Pteridium aquilinum, and Xerophyllum tenax, reflecting the mesic nature of many of these shrublands. Stands occur in foothills below treeline and as patches within the continuous forest in the montane and subalpine zones. Below treeline these shrublands typically occur within the matrix of surrounding low-elevation grasslands and sagebrush shrublands. They also occur in the ponderosa pine and Douglas-fir zones, but less commonly in the subalpine zone, where stands are often restricted to dry sites and avalanche chutes. Sites often are steep slopes of canyons, upper to middle mountain slopes, ridgetops, more commonly on sunny southern aspects. These communities also develop near talus slopes as garlands, at the heads of dry drainages, and toeslopes in the moist shrub-steppe and steppe zones. Soils are variable. Fire, flooding, avalanche, and erosion all impact these shrublands, but they typically will persist on sites for long periods.
1285582.macrogroup: Central Rocky Mountain Amelanchier alnifolia - Menziesia ferruginea - Physocarpus malvaceus Shrubland Macrogroup

1285584.scientificName: Rocky Mountain Festuca idahoensis - Festuca viridula - Muhlenbergia montana Grassland Macrogroup
1285584.conceptSentence: This grassland and meadow macrogroup is widespread in the Rocky Mountains cordillera from New Mexico and Colorado north into Canada, and west into the eastern Cascades and eastern Sierra Nevada, with outliers in the Palouse region. A diverse set of montane grasses and forbs occur across its range. These grasslands are restricted to sites where finely textured soils, snow deposition, rocky substrates, hot aspects, or windswept dry conditions limit tree establishment.
1285584.summary:
This grassland and meadow macrogroup is widespread in the Rocky Mountains cordillera from New Mexico and Colorado north into Canada, and west into the eastern Cascades and eastern Sierra Nevada. It also occurs in foothills and valleys in the Palouse Prairie region. In southern latitude stands where more summer precipitation occurs, vegetation is typically a mixture of warm and cool season grasses such as: Achnatherum lettermanii, Blepharoneuron tricholepis, Festuca arizonica, Muhlenbergia montana, Pascopyrum smithii, and several species of sedges at lower elevation / warmer aspects, or Danthonia parryi.

At subalpine elevations / cooler aspects Danthonia intermedia, Festuca idahoensis, Festuca thurberi, Poa lettermanii, or Pseudoroegneria spicata often dominate. Common subdominants include Bouteloua gracilis, Hesperostipa comata, and Poa fendleriana. In the Central Rocky Mountains, the herbaceous layer is composed of cool season bunchgrasses Achnatherum nelsonii, Calamagrostis rubescens, Elymus lanceolatus, Festuca campestris, Festuca idahoensis, Leucopoa kingii, and Pseudoroegneria spicata with many other perennial graminoid and forb species present to codominant including a variety of upland sedges. In further northern latitude stands, cool-season perennial bunchgrasses Festuca campestris and Festuca idahoensis often dominate and Pseudoroegneria spicata and Danthonia parryi frequently codominate with a diversity of other native grasses and forbs, and sometimes with a sparse (<10% cover) shrub layer. Stands in the northern Rocky Mountains and Eastern Cascades tend to be mesic often forming meadows. Meadows may be dominated by graminoids such as Carex hoodii, Elymus trachycaulus, Festuca viridula, and Phleum alpinum with occasional Dasiphora fruticosa ssp. floribunda and Symphoricarpos spp. present, but are typically forb rich with forbs often contributing more cover than graminoids. Important taxa include Balsamorhiza sagittata, Thalictrum occidentale, Valeriana sitchensis, Xerophyllum tenax, and species of Carex, Ligusticum, Lupinus, Penstemon, Pteridium, and Wyethia. In the eastern Sierra Nevada Calamagrostis breweri, Carex filifolia, Carex straminiformis, Juncus drummondii, and Trisetum spicatum may dominate stands with diagnostic forbs Oreostemma alpigenum, and Solidago canadensis. Dwarf-shrubs such as Vaccinium cespitosum may have significant cover. These grasslands are restricted to sites where finely textured soils, snow deposition, rocky substrates, hot aspects, or windswept dry conditions limit tree establishment. Many occurrences are small patch in spatial character, and are often found in mosaics with woodlands, denser shrublands, or just below alpine communities. More commonly stands occur as large patch grasslands intermixed with matrix forest stands of spruce-fir, lodgepole pine, ponderosa pine, and aspen forests. In limited circumstances (e.g., South Park in Colorado, Palouse Prairie), they form the "matrix". Stands occur over a wide latitudinal gradient so naturally have a broad elevation range. Southern latitude sites range from 2000 and 3000 m elevation occurring in montane and subalpine on flat to rolling plains and parks or on lower side slopes that are dry, extending up to 3700 m on warm aspects in subalpine. In northern stands elevations vary from 300 to 2300 m, ranging from small meadows to large open parks surrounded by conifers in the lower montane, to extensive foothill and valley grasslands below the lower treeline including the Palouse Prairie.
1285584.macrogroup: Rocky Mountain Festuca idahoensis - Festuca viridula - Muhlenbergia montana Grassland Macrogroup

1299044.scientificName: North Pacific Carex aquatilis - Carex cusickii - Carex lasiocarpa Fen Macrogroup
1299044.conceptSentence: This macrogroup contains saturated, circumneutral to alkaline (pH >5.5) fens with deep organic soils (>40 cm) that occur in the lowlands and montane zones west of the Cascades and coastal ranges from northern California to southeast Alaska. Characteristic species include Cyperaceae species such as Carex utriculata, Carex aquatilis var. dives, Carex lasiocarpa, Carex limosa, Carex cuscikii, Eleocharis quinqueflora, and Trichophorum spp. with common shrub species such as Alnus alnobetula ssp. sinuata, Betula glandulosa, Betula pumila, Myrica gale, Salix spp., and/or Spiraea douglasii.
1299044.summary: This fen type occurs in saturated, circumneutral to alkaline (pH >5.5) settings with deep organic soils (>40 cm) of the lowlands (less common) and montane zones west of the Cascades and coastal ranges from northern California to southeast Alaska. This includes circumneutral fens, which are the most common type while rich / extremely rich (alkaline) fens that are associated with ultra-mafic and calcareous bedrocks outcrops (serpentine fens and calcareous fens, respectively) are rarer. Tree cover ranges from absent to open woodlands. Lowland fens often lack trees while montane or northerly fens can include Callitropsis nootkatensis (= Chamaecyparis nootkatensis), Pinus contorta, Thuja plicata, and/or Tsuga heterophylla. Herbaceous vegetation is primarily comprised of various species of Carex, with Carex utriculata, Carex aquatilis var. dives, Carex lasiocarpa, Carex limosa, and Carex cuscikii being common. Eleocharis quinqueflora and Trichophorum spp. are also commonly found and occasionally dominant. Caltha leptosepala, Comarum palustre and Menyanthes trifoliata are common around pools, pond edges, or other areas with deeper water. Brown mosses such as Aulocomnium palustre, Campylium, Drepanocladus, Scorpidium, Tomentypnum, and Warnstorfia are characteristic but not always dominant.
1299044.macrogroup: North Pacific Carex aquatilis - Carex cusickii - Carex lasiocarpa Fen Macrogroup

1309263.scientificName: Juniperus horizontalis - Arctostaphylos uva-ursi / Calamovilfa longifolia Sand Dwarf-shrubland Alliance
1309263.primaryCommonName: Northern Plains Low Shrub Sand Prairie
1309263.conceptSentence: This northern Great Plains prairie shrubland alliance is found on sandy sites in the Canadian prairie provinces and is characterized more by topography and soils than common dominant shrub species, which may include Juniperus horizontalis, Arctostaphylos uva-ursi, Prunus virginiana, Rosa woodsii, Artemisia cana, or Symphoricarpos occidentalis. Sporobolus rigidus is almost always present.
1309263.summary: This Northern Great Plains prairie shrubland alliance occurs on sandy sites in the dry and mesic grassland areas of the Canadian Prairie Provinces. It is commonly dominated by Juniperus horizontalis and Arctostaphylos uva-ursi, but can also be dominated by Prunus virginiana, Rosa woodsii, Artemisia cana, or Symphoricarpos occidentalis. A sparse tree layer of Betula occidentalis may occur infrequently in sites that are subirrigated. Sporobolus rigidus is almost always present but with low cover. Other graminoids include Carex inops, Carex obtusata, Carex siccata, Hesperostipa comata, and Festuca saximontana. Festuca hallii may be present in the Parklands of Alberta. The dwarf-shrub Artemisia frigida is common and prominent forbs include Heterotheca villosa, Psoralidium lanceolatum, Solidago missouriensis and Thermopsis rhombifolia. Juniperus horizontalis increases in the absence of fire and is eliminated by it. This alliance can revert to a plant community where Juniperus horizontalis is replaced by Hesperostipa comata, Elymus lanceolatus, and Carex spp. It occurs on unstable sand soils and on low and mid-relief dunes and is more reflective of the topography and soils rather than consistent dominant species across sites.
